title: AI and the Opportunity for Shared Prosperity: Lessons From the History of Technology and the Economy

statistical_results: AI in one study creating significant value over existing analytical tools in 69% of business use-cases and emerging as the sole analytical solution in an additional 15%

a recent study that aggregated survey data across all U.S. occupations found that one-fourth of work tasks could be automated by AI, with particularly significant opportunities in administrative (46%) and legal (44%) professions
a similar study concluded that AI can automate tasks associated with 30% of hours worked across the U.S. economy
McKinsey Global Institute estimates that AI (including generative AI) could deliver $17 to $25 trillion annually to the global economy
Goldman Sachs projects that AI could increase global GDP by 7%
call centers reported an improvement of 14% in issues resolved per hour
developers completed assigned tasks 55% faster
AI-assisted financial analysts outperformed other human analysts in 57% of the forecasts
businesses that used relatively basic versions of AI grew revenues 40% faster and employment 25% faster than those that did not
McKinsey Global Institute estimated that generative AI could increase U.S. labor productivity by 0.5% to 0.9% a year (relative to an average annual growth of 1.4%)
Goldman Sachs estimates that generative AI alone could boost U.S. labor productivity by 1.5 percentage points a year
the majority of employees using AI at work reported that in addition to being more productive, AI had also improved their enjoyment of work (63%) and overall mental health (54%)
U.S. productivity growth, which averaged 1.7% in the decade leading to the financial crisis, dropped to 0.5% in the decade before the pandemic
large services sectors—which account for nearly 80% of U.S. employment—achieved pre-pandemic productivity growth of just 0.16%
over the past decade aging economies such as China (18%), the U.S. (9%) and the OECD (8%) have experienced this phenomenon
within a single decade internet access was expanded to 2.3 billion people
the developing world, which accounted for only a third of the digital population two decades ago, today stands at about 72%
nearly three billion people are either offline or do not have sufficient access to take advantage of the opportunities today
more than 90% of people in high-income countries used the internet in 2022, only one in four in low-income countries have access
Africa 60%, Asia Pacific 36% of the population having either unreliable, unaffordable or no access at all to the internet
PwC estimates that nearly 70% of economic value generated by AI will accrue to just two countries: the U.S. and China
AlphaFold's protein predictions have been accessed by more than 1.6 million researchers in more than 190 countries
GNoME enabled the discovery of 2.2 million new crystals, equivalent to nearly 800 years' worth of knowledge
AI-based predictions to reduce contrails by as much as 54%
AI has shown the ability to increase graduation rates at a liberal arts college by 32% through tracking students' grades and course selection
between 1910 and 1950, over half a million jobs were displaced by automobiles

automobiles are estimated to have generated a staggering 7.5 million jobs, which are not only roughly tenfold the amount of jobs that they displaced, but also an estimated 10% of employment at that time
employment for typists, typewriter manufacturers and secretaries fell significantly following the advent of personal computers, ultimately leading to a loss of 3.5 million jobs
at least 19 million jobs were actually created between 1970 and 2015 in a wide range of new and existing occupations
60% of the U.S. workforce today is in occupations that did not exist eighty years ago
agricultural share of total employment declined from 60% in 1850 to less than 5% by 1970
employment in manufacturing fell from 26% in 1960 to less than 10% today
of the 270 occupations listed in the 1950 U.S. Census, only one, elevator operator, was eliminated due to automation
ambiguity_or_caveats: While we do not presume the future will be entirely like that past – for reasons we will discuss – we do believe prior experience with technological change offers many useful lessons
While there is a lot that we must collectively get right about AI, including issues of safety and responsible AI development, in this paper, we focus on the economy and shared prosperity

While improvements in AI have been significant, current AI systems, especially LLM-based systems, have some performance and other limitations with respect to factuality and hallucinations, and in some cases safety, or capability gaps with respect to memory and planning that affect their broad applicability. These however are all areas of ongoing research.

To clarify the analysis above, we recognize that the threshold for becoming a general-purpose technology is understandably high. And although there have been plenty of candidates over the years (e.g., blockchain, nanotechnology and the internet of things), many of these have so far proved to be limited in their generality.
But such estimated gains are not automatic as we will discuss later
However, while there are potential opportunities for the Global South, major gaps will need to be addressed

Moreover, as we explained below, because access depends on investments spanning a multitude of dimensions (e.g. digital literacy, data availability, and reliable electricity) resolving such divides presents a multidimensional challenge

Yet, even if AI creates enough net new jobs to offset those that it may displace, some workers may lose their jobs during the transition, need to adapt to AI-assisted work, or need to find new jobs altogether. And while AI may eventually create better jobs, there is no guarantee that displaced workers would be reemployed, have the right skills, or that better jobs would emerge in time.
Suchtechnological disruptionanduncertaintyaboutthenatureoffuturejobscanexacerbatethesechallenges
Empirical evidence however suggests that such prospects are often uncertain and ineffective at raising long-term compensation
While technology and other market economy innovations have driven tremendous increases in prosperity over the last century, the benefits have not been evenly distributed
Skill-biased technological progress often led to disproportionate benefits owing to more educated and more skilled workers

In fact, the impact of AI on white-collar, blue-collar, and "new-collar" jobs remains uncertain – just as industrialization both complemented and substituted for some blue-collar jobs, AI may have similar effects on white-collar employment.
However, many have been quick to point out that these gains will not happen automatically, a topic we will discuss in Chapter 2 of this paper.

While the concerns over displacement were evident and genuine at the time, it was probably difficult to anticipate the tremendous job creation that automobiles would eventually stimulate in subsequent years.

However, while there are many reasons to be optimistic about AI's role as a catalyst for better jobs and what The Economist recently described as "a golden age for workers", given technology's current interplay with jobs and the state of the labor markets in most advanced economies, we also recognize that the challenges discussed earlier – of workforce readiness, transitions, possibility of displacement – are significant and important to tackle. Moreover, AI may introduce new challenges, such as the possibility of change occurring faster than in previous eras.

As discussed above, the challenges detailed above must be addressed, including supporting SMBs in process and organizational changes, developing products that are reliable for wide use, promoting human-complementary AI which does not prioritize substitution, ensuring that digital infrastructure is complete, and that workers have access to effective, practical vocational programs

While we are optimistic about AI's potential to promote the mechanisms of economic progress described above, we also want to highlight the possibility of AI benefits that we simply cannot envision today.
As optimistic as we are about AI's incredible economic potential, we also recognize that realizing it is not automatic or guaranteed.

While there is a lot that we must collectively get right about AI, including issues of safety and responsible AI development, in this paper, we focus on the economy and shared prosperity and explore four key questions
While the Wright brothers pioneered the first manned flight in 1903, it took another half a century for commercial aviation to become widely available.
While Edison demonstrated the electric light bulb in 1879, it would take another four decades before most homes became electrified and were able to install light bulbs.

While a technology can seem to be everywhere all at once, until it has been adopted and used broadly in productivity-enhancing and opportunity-expanding ways across large swathes of the economy, overall economic impact and prosperity will not be fully realized.

Technological change has of course dramatically accelerated since Edison's light bulb or the Wright brothers historic flight. But they serve as vivid reminders that transformative technologies often face persistent challenges that can take time and collective action to overcome.

there are clear lessons from the past and insights from recent studies on AI's potential, however, it's important to note that many of the empirical case studies are still early and limited in scope – therefore more research is needed especially as AI continues to develop, and more is learned from its deployment, use and impact.

notes: This is a position/policy paper by Google executives and affiliated economists. It is not an empirical research study with original experiments. The paper synthesizes historical evidence about technology adoption and cites numerous case studies and research findings from other sources to argue that AI presents economic opportunity but requires deliberate policy action. The statistical results quoted are drawn from cited studies (McKinsey, Goldman Sachs, OECD, etc.) rather than original analysis by the authors. The paper focuses on economic opportunity and does not address safety, fairness, or other non-economic dimensions of AI in depth.
